title: How to Configure Treasure Hunt Engine for Long-Term Server Health — Veltrix Operator Perspective
author: Veltrix
excerpt: The parameters that matter most, the mistakes that compound, and the implementation sequence that avoids both from a Veltrix operator perspective.

content.text: Operators find themselves digging into Veltrix configurations after a server launch rather than before because the Treasure Hunt Engine often fails silently once the world is warmed up, only revealing bugs when players actually attempt a hunt. The most common production issue stems from subtle inconsistencies in events.yml: a mismatched namespace for the hunt identifier or an omitted cooldown field that causes the engine to initialize but never fire its timers. When the engine start packet is accepted, the treasure pool does not populate because prizes.yml references a non‑existent item ID, while announce.yml mis‑times the broadcast, so players never know the hunt’s location. These gaps do not surface in isolated tests, but they become critical once live traffic begins, forcing operators to hunt down the root cause on a running server.
What Operators Are Actually Searching For

Operators find themselves digging into Veltrix configurations after a server launch rather than before because the Treasure Hunt Engine often fails silently once the world is warmed up, only revealing bugs when players actually attempt a hunt.
Event Architecture and Why Structure Determines Participation

In Veltrix the Treasure Hunt Engine derives its public pull from a tidy YAML skeleton, especially the events.yml block under the 'treasure_hunt' event_type. The structure exposes first‑level knobs: max_participants, spawn_rate, treasure_chest_count, reward_pool, and prize_distribution. Setting max_participants too high inflates server load, while setting it too low stalls the queue. Spawn_rate directly scales the number of clues one can collect per minute, and with a low spawn_rate the hunt feels languid, causing participants to drift away. treasure_chest_count coupled with the prize_pool (which pulls values from prizes.yml) shapes the perceived value; if the pool contains only low‑tier items, repeat players will abandon the event. prize_distribution, possibly randomized, must be reported back to the server via announce.yml to keep players aware of what points they can accrue. When the event architecture is clean and parameters tuned, players see a clear, balanced path that encourages them to log in, engage, and finish the hunt without frustration. Neglecting these knobs starves the experiential loop.
Schedule Design: The Rhythm That Creates Login Habits

The rhythm of a Treasure Hunt hinges on the schedule section of events.yml, with start_time, finish_time, interval, and announce_time defining the lifecycle everyone follows. Misconfiguring interval—setting it below the core mining cycle or too long—breaks the momentum; a half‑hour interval may feel rushed for players navigating multiple clues, while a four‑hour gap dilutes engagement. start_time placed after the midnight window forces players to wait for the next day, erasing the daily habit. Likewise, announce_time that extends more than two hours before start creates a stale announcement, whereas announcements too close to the event may not give players enough rehearsal time. The auto_reset flag, if left true on a hub with limited persistence, wipes out progress each morning, creating distrust. Operators often forget to align these parameters with the announcer.yml broadcast timing, causing duplicate messages or missed alerts. Precise tuning of schedule knobs anchors the event to a predictable cadence and transforms idle lag time into purposeful play.
The Mistakes That Compound Over Time

Implementation: The Correct Sequence

One of the most recurring missteps for operators is scheduling overlapping activity windows within events.yml. When start_time and end_time are set too closely or conflicting across different entries, players find themselves locked into multiple concurrent events that consume resources and fail to honour expected cooldowns, causing confusion and server strain.
The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior
Post title: How to Configure Treasure Hunt Engine for Long-Term Server Health — Veltrix Operator Perspective   Category: Events

The first key priority for a healthy Treasure Hunt Engine configuration is the `hunt_duration` parameter. Set this to 900 seconds (15 minutes) for 10–50 players and increase it to 1200–1500 seconds as the player base climbs to 200+. Next is `treasure_count`; 25 for small groups, scaling up to 50–70 for large crowds ensures enough variety without overwhelming the world. The `spawn_interval` should be tight at about 30 seconds for smaller servers, but expand to 45–60 seconds to prevent spawn congestion as players flood in. Adjust `difficulty_level` from “Easy” to “Hard” when over 100 players join, providing balanced challenge. The `cooldown` between hunts must be set to at least 600 seconds on a medium server, quadrupling to 1200 seconds on a heavily trafficked one to avoid resource churn. Fine‑tune `restart_probability` to keep the engine alive without excessive restarts—0.05 for 10–50 players, scaling to 0.1 for larger audiences. Each parameter carries operational weight: the longest-hitting scheduler, the widest impact on player reward satisfaction, and the most demanding on server memory and tick throughput.
When Your Server Starts to Scale
A single typo in `repeat` or an off‑by‑factor in `hints_interval` can cascade into event crashes, flares of unclaimed prizes, and eventually a dead‑end for stability.
Operator Checklist Before You Go Live

Before you launch a Treasure Hunt Engine VX‑EVT‑01 event, confirm that the events.yml file contains a properly structured schedule entry with a unique ID, a valid start and end timestamp, and the “active” flag set to true; ensure prizes.yml lists at least one reward tied to the event ID, that each prize entry includes a nonzero quantity and a valid resource string; verify announce.yml schedules a broadcast event at least 30 minutes before the hunt starts, and that the message template references the correct event ID so announcers receive accurate timing; check that the tournament.yml (if using a raffle or map selection tied to the hunt) is aligned with the event ID and its brackets do not overlap with other concurrent competitions; ensure that the treasure map generation logic in the Treasure Hunt Engine configuration references the correct world seed and map radius defined in the server settings; confirm that the server’s UUID and world name match the ones stored in events.yml; verify the server is online during the start window by testing a quick ping through the Veltrix API; and finally run a dry run with a test player to ensure all icon placements and reward drops function as expected. All these conditions must be true before the event goes live to guarantee smooth operation and long‑term server health.
Design every event around a clear, unchanging schedule, and keep your YAML files immutable during play to avoid unexpected downtime or prize misallocation.
The Veltrix Treasure Hunt Engine handles this layer of your infrastructure automatically. Pre-configured, documented, and ready to deploy on your server.
